h1Title: Autoimmune Polyglandular Failure: Symptoms, Types & Management
heroOverview: Autoimmune polyglandular failure (also called autoimmune polyglandular syndrome or APS) refers to the combination of autoimmune-mediated failure of two or more endocrine glands in the same patient. APS type 1 (OMIM 240300), caused by AIRE gene mutations, is rare (prevalence 1 in 25,000) and presents in childhood with the classic triad of chronic candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison disease. APS type 2 (OMIM 269200) is more common and presents in adults aged 30-50 with Addison disease, autoimmune thyroid disease, and/or Type 1 diabetes, often in combination. The condition results from loss of immune tolerance to self-antigens shared across endocrine tissues, driven by CTLA4, HLA, and PTPN22 gene variants. Management is complex, requiring replacement of all deficient hormones and vigilant surveillance for new glandular failures that develop over years. Without treatment, adrenal crisis and hypocalcaemic tetany are life-threatening complications.

keyStats.prevalence: APS type 1: 1 in 25,000 globally; APS type 2: 1 in 5,000-10,000. Addison disease coexists with thyroid disease in 10-15% of autoimmune adrenal insufficiency cases, and with T1DM in 10-12%
keyStats.demographics: APS type 1: childhood onset (usually <5 years), equal sex ratio; APS type 2: adult onset (30-50 years), female predominance (F:M 3-4:1)
keyStats.avgAge: APS type 1 median onset 5-7 years; APS type 2 median onset 35-45 years
keyStats.globalCases: APS type 1 worldwide; APS type 2 more common in Northern Europe and North America

definition: Autoimmune polyglandular failure (ICD-10: E31.0) is defined as the autoimmune-mediated destruction of two or more endocrine glands in the same individual. APS type 1 (APS-1, autoimmune polyendocrinopathy-candidiasis-ectodermal dystrophy or APECED) is caused by autosomal recessive mutations in the AIRE gene (21q22.3), which encodes a transcription factor essential for central immune tolerance. AIRE deficiency allows autoreactive T cells to escape thymic deletion and attack endocrine tissues. The classic triad is chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (CMC, present in 100%), hypoparathyroidism (60-90%), and Addison disease (50-80%). APS type 2 (Schmidt syndrome) is polygenic, driven by HLA-DR3/DQ2 and CTLA4 risk alleles, and presents in adults with Addison disease, autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto or Graves), and/or Type 1 diabetes mellitus. Both conditions require lifelong hormone replacement and surveillance for emerging gland failures. Diagnosis of APS type 1 is confirmed by AIRE gene sequencing or detection of anti-IFN-alpha and anti-IFN-omega autoantibodies.

typesClassification.type: APS type 1 (APECED) — AIRE gene mutations, childhood onset
typesClassification.description: Caused by biallelic AIRE mutations; presents with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison disease; may also have gonadal failure, type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, alopecia, and pernicious anaemia.

typesClassification.type: APS type 2 (Schmidt syndrome) — autoimmune, adult onset
typesClassification.description: Polygenic with HLA-DR3/DQ2 association; Addison disease combined with autoimmune thyroid disease (most common) and/or Type 1 diabetes. Female predominance.

typesClassification.type: APS type 3 — autoimmune thyroid disease plus another autoimmune disease, without Addison disease
typesClassification.description: Includes combinations such as autoimmune thyroid disease with T1DM, pernicious anaemia, or vitiligo, without adrenal involvement.

typesClassification.type: APS type 4 — multiple autoimmune conditions without classic triad
typesClassification.description: Various combinations of autoimmune conditions; less well-defined entity; clinically important for surveillance.
Rare immune dysregulation syndrome with early-onset Type 1 diabetes, enteropathy, eczema, and susceptibility to infections; distinct from APS type 1.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue, hyperpigmentation of skin creases and mucous membranes, salt craving, and orthostatic hypotension from autoimmune Addison disease (adrenal insufficiency).
Muscle cramps, perioral paraesthesia, and Chvostek or Trousseau signs from hypocalcaemia due to autoimmune hypoparathyroidism (APS type 1).
Chronic white patches in the mouth and oesophagus from chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (APS type 1, often the presenting feature).
Hypothyroid symptoms in autoimmune thyroiditis: cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, weight gain, and bradycardia.
Hyperthyroid symptoms in Graves disease overlap: heat intolerance, weight loss, tremor, anxiety, and palpitations.
Polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss from autoimmune Type 1 diabetes mellitus.
Amenorrhoea, hot flushes, and vaginal dryness from autoimmune ovarian or testicular failure.
Vitiligo (depigmented skin patches) or premature hair greying from melanocyte destruction.
Gastrointestinal symptoms from pernicious anaemia (B12 malabsorption) with megaloblastic anaemia and neuropathy.
Chronic diarrhoea from autoimmune enteropathy (APS type 1).
earlyWarningSigns: New endocrine deficiency emerging in a patient with known autoimmune endocrine disease
Salt craving or hyperpigmentation in a patient with Type 1 diabetes or thyroid disease
Recurrent candidiasis in a child with autoimmune family history
Unexplained hypocalcaemia in a patient with other autoimmune diseases
Autoantibody positivity (anti-21-hydroxylase, anti-thyroid peroxidase, anti-GAD65) on screening in family members of APS patients
Persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, or failure to thrive in a child with known APS type 1
emergencySigns: Adrenal crisis with profound hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, and shock — requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100mg and aggressive fluid resuscitation
Severe hypocalcaemia with tetany, laryngospasm, seizures, or cardiac arrhythmias — requires urgent IV calcium gluconate
Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state or diabetic ketoacidosis in APS patients with new-onset Type 1 diabetes
Severe oral or oesophageal candidiasis with dysphagia and weight loss — requires systemic antifungal therapy
Severe autoimmune hepatitis with jaundice, coagulopathy, and hepatic encephalopathy — requires urgent hepatology input

causes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function mutations in the AIRE gene (21q22.3) disrupt central immune tolerance. AIRE promotes thymic expression of tissue-specific self-antigens; without it, autoreactive T cells escape deletion and attack endocrine tissues. Over 100 AIRE mutations are known; the most common in Northern Europeans is R257X.

APS type 2 results from cumulative effect of multiple autoimmune risk alleles including HLA-DR3/DQ2, CTLA4, PTPN22, and CLEC16A. These variants alter T-cell regulatory function, HLA antigen presentation, and peripheral tolerance, predisposing to multiple autoimmune endocrine diseases.

Common target antigens (21-hydroxylase in adrenal cortex, thyroid peroxidase, GAD65 in beta cells, and tissue transglutaminase) are expressed across endocrine organs, explaining why autoimmune damage clusters in specific organ combinations. Autoantibodies against these antigens precede clinical disease.

APS type 1 is autosomal recessive (AIRE mutations). APS type 2 is not inherited in a simple Mendelian pattern but has strong familial clustering; first-degree relatives of APS type 2 patients have a 1-5% risk of developing any autoimmune disease.

causes.cause: Environmental triggers in genetically susceptible individuals
causes.description: Infections (particularly enteroviruses), vitamin D deficiency, and stress may trigger autoimmune endocrine disease onset in genetically susceptible individuals. Interferon-alpha therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors can also induce APS-like syndromes.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of autoimmune endocrine disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of APS type 2 patients have elevated risk of developing autoimmune thyroid disease, T1DM, or Addison disease.
Biallelic AIRE mutations confer near-complete penetrance for APS type 1 by adulthood.
HLA-DR3 and DQ2 are strongly associated with APS type 2, particularly Addison plus thyroid disease combinations.
Female predominance (3-4:1) in APS type 2 reflects the higher prevalence of autoimmune disease in women.
Higher prevalence in Northern Europe and North America; APS type 1 is enriched in Finnish, Sardinian, and Iranian Jewish populations.
Presence of anti-21-hydroxylase, anti-TPO, anti-GAD65, or anti-IFN antibodies indicates ongoing autoimmune activity and predicts future glandular failure.

affectedDemographics: APS type 1 presents in childhood, most often before age 5; median age of first manifestation 2-5 years.
APS type 2 presents in adulthood, typically between ages 30-50; female predominance.
APS type 1 affects all ethnic groups equally; APS type 2 more common in Northern European and North American populations.
APS type 2 clusters in families; affected siblings are common.
APS type 1 is rare but more severe; APS type 2 is more common but milder organ involvement.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of APS is considered when two or more autoimmune endocrine conditions coexist in the same patient. Clinical assessment includes history (symptoms of hormone deficiency), examination (pigmentation, thyroid examination, skin vitiligo), and laboratory testing. Initial work-up includes: ACTH and cortisol (for Addison disease), 08:00 ACTH stimulation test if equivocal; TSH, free T4, free T3, anti-TPO antibodies (for autoimmune thyroid disease); HbA1c, fasting glucose, anti-GAD65, anti-islet cell antibodies (for T1DM); calcium, phosphate, PTH, 25-OH vitamin D (for hypoparathyroidism); LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone (for gonadal failure); full blood count, B12, folate, anti-intrinsic factor, anti-tissue transglutaminase (for pernicious anaemia). For suspected APS type 1, AIRE gene sequencing and anti-IFN-alpha/omega antibody testing (positive in >95% of APS type 1 patients) confirm the diagnosis. Annual surveillance screening for emerging glandular failures is mandatory once APS is diagnosed.

diagnosticTests.test: ACTH and cortisol with ACTH stimulation test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for autoimmune Addison disease; low morning cortisol with elevated ACTH and blunted cortisol response to synthetic ACTH confirms adrenal insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood tests at 08:00 and 30/60 minutes after IV cosyntropin 250µg; results within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: TSH, free T4, free T3, anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto or Graves); anti-TPO positive in 90% of Hashimoto cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses autoimmune Type 1 diabetes; anti-GAD65 positive in 70-80% of T1DM at diagnosis
Diagnoses hypoparathyroidism (low calcium, high phosphate, low/inappropriately normal PTH); characteristic of APS type 1
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms APS type 1; identifies biallelic pathogenic variants; cascade testing for family members
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood for DNA; 3-4 weeks for results; targeted panel or whole-exome sequencing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highly specific (>95%) for APS type 1; used as diagnostic marker when AIRE sequencing is unavailable
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialized blood test; results in 1-2 weeks at reference laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Predicts development of autoimmune Addison disease; positive in 60-80% of patients with APS and impending adrenal failure

diagnosticTests.test: Screening for associated conditions: FBC, B12, LFTs, TFTs annually
diagnosticTests.purpose: Longitudinal monitoring for emerging glandular failures and associated autoimmune diseases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Annual battery of blood tests; results within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment involves replacement of all deficient hormones and vigilant monitoring for new glandular failures. Adrenal insufficiency requires hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses and fludrocortisone 50-200 µg/day; stress dosing and emergency hydrocortisone injection are essential education points. Hypoparathyroidism requires calcium supplements and active vitamin D analogues (calcitriol or alfacalcidol); target calcium 2.0-2.5 mmol/L. Hypothyroidism requires levothyroxine replacement. Type 1 diabetes requires insulin therapy (multiple daily injections or insulin pump). Chronic candidiasis requires oral azoles (itraconazole, fluconazole) as suppressive therapy. Hormone replacement for gonadal failure (testosterone, oestrogen/progesterone) supports bone health, sexual function, and quality of life. Pernicious anaemia requires intramuscular B12 injections. Immunosuppressive therapy is not indicated for endocrine gland replacement, but novel antigen-specific immunotherapy and B-cell depletion (rituximab) are under study for APS type 1.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone 10-5-5 mg (or 20-10 mg) in divided daily doses for adrenal replacement
medicalTreatments.description: Physiological glucocorticoid replacement for Addison disease; given in 2-3 divided doses; dose doubled or tripled for physiological stress (illness, surgery).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects glucocorticoid deficiency; prevents adrenal crisis; doses must be increased 2-3 fold during illness or procedures

medicalTreatments.name: Fludrocortisone 50-200 µg once daily for mineralocorticoid replacement
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic mineralocorticoid for Addison disease; maintains salt balance and blood pressure; not needed if hydrocortisone dose is >50mg/day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains normal blood pressure and electrolyte balance; monitor BP and potassium

medicalTreatments.test: Calcium supplements (1-3 g/day elemental calcium) + calcitriol 0.25-2 µg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Standard hypoparathyroidism treatment; targets serum calcium 2.0-2.5 mmol/L to prevent tetany and nephrocalcinosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents hypocalcaemic symptoms; requires regular calcium and creatinine monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine 50-150 µg/day (weight-based)
medicalTreatments.description: Thyroid hormone replacement for autoimmune hypothyroidism; dose titrated to maintain TSH in normal range.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises TSH and resolves hypothyroid symptoms; takes 6-8 weeks for full effect after dose change

medicalTreatments.name: Multiple daily insulin injection (MDI) or insulin pump for Type 1 diabetes
medicalTreatments.description: Basal-bolus insulin regimen (long-acting plus rapid-acting insulin) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves target HbA1c and prevents acute and chronic complications of diabetes

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole 200 mg once daily or fluconazole 100 mg daily for chronic candidiasis
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term azole antifungal suppression; rotates or combines agents to reduce resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Suppresses candidiasis in 70-80%; resistance may develop requiring alternative agents

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1 mg every 3 months for pernicious anaemia
medicalTreatments.description: B12 replacement bypassing the need for intrinsic factor; lifelong therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects B12 deficiency; reverses megaloblastic anaemia and prevents neurological complications

medicalTreatments.name: Testosterone or oestrogen/progesterone replacement for gonadal failure
medicalTreatments.description: Physiological sex hormone replacement for premature ovarian or testicular failure; supports bone density, sexual function, and quality of life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains bone density and sexual characteristics; improves energy and quality of life

surgicalOptions.name: Parathyroidectomy is not indicated for hypoparathyroidism management
surgicalOptions.description: Surgery plays no role in managing autoimmune hypoparathyroidism. Autotransplantation of parathyroid tissue at time of thyroid surgery is occasionally performed but is not relevant to APS.

surgicalOptions.name: Thyroidectomy for compressive or malignant thyroid disease (if applicable)
surgicalOptions.description: May be required if Graves disease is refractory to medical therapy or if thyroid nodules are malignant. Lifelong levothyroxine replacement is mandatory.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curative for thyroid cancer or refractory Graves; requires subsequent thyroid hormone replacement

alternativeTreatments.name: PTH replacement therapy (Natpara) for hypoparathyroidism
alternativeTreatments.description: Recombinant human PTH (teriparatide) or rhPTH(1-84) (Natpara) is approved for chronic hypoparathyroidism; reduces calcium and vitamin D requirements.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces calcium and vitamin D supplement burden; FDA REMS programme required due to osteosarcoma risk in rodents; not widely available

alternativeTreatments.name: Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (insulin pump) for Type 1 diabetes
alternativeTreatments.description: Insulin pump provides continuous basal insulin with meal-time boluses; may improve glycaemic control and reduce hypoglycaemia in motivated patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-dose ACTH therapy (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-dose depot ACTH injections have been tried for autoimmune adrenalitis in APS type 1; evidence is limited.

whySeeSpecialist: APS requires expert endocrinology management to coordinate multiple hormone replacements, monitor interactions between treatments (e.g., glucocorticoid and thyroid hormone), and screen for new glandular failures. An endocrinologist with autoimmune endocrine experience is essential for diagnosis, initial treatment design, and ongoing surveillance.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinology centre with specific APS experience, access to all relevant endocrine subspecialties (adrenal, thyroid, diabetes), and support from immunology and genetics. Ask about their APS patient volume, screening protocols, and experience with adrenal crisis management.
hospitalCriteria: Endocrinology service with adrenal, thyroid, and diabetes expertise
Genetics service for AIRE testing and family counselling
Emergency department staff trained in adrenal crisis management
Laboratory with endocrine testing including ACTH stimulation and antibody panels
keyFacilities: Diabetes specialist nurse for T1DM in APS
Clinical genetics for APS type 1 families
Emergency protocols for adrenal crisis and hypocalcaemia
preventionStrategies: Screening for subclinical glandular failure using autoantibody panels (anti-21-hydroxylase, anti-TPO, anti-GAD65) in patients with known APS or first-degree relatives of APS type 1 patients.
Annual endocrine surveillance with ACTH stimulation test, thyroid function, HbA1c, calcium/PTH, and gonadal hormones in all APS patients.
Prophylactic adrenal hormone replacement before onset of clinical Addison disease in anti-21-hydroxylase antibody-positive patients with impending adrenal failure.
Pre-conception counselling and close maternal-foetal monitoring in APS patients planning pregnancy.
Stress-dose steroid education and emergency hydrocortisone injection kit for all patients with adrenal insufficiency.
lifestyleModifications: Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying adrenal insufficiency; carry an emergency hydrocortisone injection at all times.
Double or triple glucocorticoid dose during illness, fever, dental procedures, or surgery; have a sick-day rules plan from your endocrinologist.
Regular meals with adequate salt intake; fludrocortisone dose may need adjustment in hot weather or during exercise.
Engage in regular weight-bearing exercise to support bone density in patients with hypoparathyroidism and gonadal failure.
Attend all scheduled endocrine follow-up appointments for monitoring and early detection of new deficiencies.

recommended: High-sodium diet (especially with adrenal insufficiency); add salt to meals if on fludrocortisone
Calcium-rich diet (dairy, leafy greens) to complement calcium supplementation in hypoparathyroidism
Balanced diet with adequate vitamin D (sun exposure, fortified foods, or supplementation) to support bone health
Carbohydrate-counting education for patients with Type 1 diabetes
avoid: Excessive potassium-rich foods only if hyperkalaemia is present (usually not necessary with adequate fludrocortisone)
Grapefruit juice with itraconazole (CYP3A4 interaction); contraindicated
Raw licorice (glycyrrhizin) which enhances mineralocorticoid effect and can cause hypertension and hypokalaemia
Restrictive diets without medical supervision in patients with multiple endocrine deficiencies

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged for all APS patients. Patients with adrenal insufficiency require stress-dose steroids before strenuous exercise. Patients with hypoparathyroidism should maintain adequate calcium before vigorous exercise. Insulin dosing for exercise in T1DM requires specialist education to avoid hypoglycaemia.
dailyManagement: Take all replacement hormones consistently at prescribed times each day.
Double glucocorticoid dose during illness (fever >38°C, infection, injury) and contact medical team for guidance.
Carry emergency hydrocortisone injection and medical alert bracelet at all times for adrenal insufficiency.
Monitor blood glucose if diabetic; adjust insulin for meals and activity.
Report new symptoms suggesting a new glandular deficiency to the endocrinology team promptly.
Keep a medication list and adrenal crisis action plan with you at all times.
prognosis: With appropriate hormone replacement and surveillance, the prognosis for APS patients has improved dramatically. Life expectancy approaches normal with well-managed adrenal insufficiency, diabetes, and hypoparathyroidism. However, APS type 1 carries a 20-30% mortality rate in childhood and adolescence, largely from adrenal crisis, hypocalcaemia, and severe infections. APS type 2 has a near-normal life expectancy with optimal management. The main determinants of outcome are: early diagnosis of Addison disease before crisis; adequate glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement adherence; good glycaemic control in T1DM; and vigilant surveillance for new glandular failures. Quality of life is generally good with a well-managed regimen.
recoveryTimeline: APS is a lifelong condition. Each glandular failure requires weeks to months to stabilize on replacement therapy. Adrenal insufficiency requires continuous replacement with stress-dosing protocols for life. Hypoparathyroidism is managed chronically with calcium and vitamin D analogues. Once established on a stable regimen, follow-up is every 3-12 months depending on clinical stability.
complications: Adrenal crisis with cardiovascular collapse, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, and shock — prevented with adequate replacement and sick-day dosing; requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100mg.
Severe hypocalcaemia with tetany, laryngospasm, seizures, QT-prolongation arrhythmias, and cardiac arrest — prevented with adequate calcium and vitamin D monitoring.
Diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state in APS patients with Type 1 diabetes — requires urgent insulin and fluid management.
Nephrocalcinosis and chronic kidney disease from hypercalciuria in hypoparathyroidism — prevented with adequate hydration and target calcium monitoring.
Osteoporosis and fragility fractures from chronic hypoparathyroidism and hypogonadism — managed with adequate calcium, vitamin D, and sex hormone replacement.
Invasive fungal infection from chronic candidiasis spreading to oesophagus or lungs — requires systemic antifungal therapy.
Autoimmune hepatitis or hepatitis in APS type 1 — requires urgent hepatology referral; may respond to immunosuppression.
Malignancy risk (oral, oesophageal SCC) associated with chronic candidiasis in APS type 1 — surveillance with endoscopy.

supportResources.name: The Addison's Disease Self-Help Group (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting patients with adrenal insufficiency; provides emergency cards, education, and peer support.

supportResources.name: National Adrenal Diseases Foundation (US)
supportResources.description: US organization providing education and support for Addison disease and adrenal insufficiency.

supportResources.name: Diabetes UK — Type 1 diabetes
supportResources.description: Comprehensive UK resource for Type 1 diabetes management and insulin therapy.

supportResources.name: APECED Syndrome / ALMS connection patient groups
supportResources.description: International patient network for APECED (APS type 1); connects families and shares management experiences.

confusedWithConditions.name: Addison's Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Addison disease alone involves only the adrenal glands. APS involves Addison disease plus one or more additional autoimmune endocrine deficiencies. Testing for anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies and screening for thyroid, pancreatic, and parathyroid dysfunction distinguishes APS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: T1DM alone involves pancreatic beta cells. APS type 2 combines T1DM with autoimmune thyroid disease and/or Addison disease. Screening for anti-TPO and anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies identifies APS combinations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hashimoto's Thyroiditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hashimoto alone involves autoimmune thyroiditis. APS type 2 combines Hashimoto with Addison disease and/or T1DM. Addison screening (ACTH stimulation test) in all Hashimoto patients is warranted.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypoparathyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated hypoparathyroidism is usually genetic (DiGeorge) or post-surgical. Hypoparathyroidism in the context of APS type 1 occurs alongside mucocutaneous candidiasis, Addison disease, and other autoimmune features.

relatedConditions.name: Addison's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune adrenalitis is the most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency in developed countries and is a key component of both APS type 1 and type 2.

relatedConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune T1DM is part of APS type 2; anti-GAD65 antibodies distinguish autoimmune from other causes of diabetes.

relatedConditions.name: Hashimoto's Thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroiditis is the most common additional condition in APS type 2; may present as hypothyroidism or rarely as Graves disease.

relatedConditions.name: Hypoparathyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Characteristic of APS type 1 (along with candidiasis and Addison disease); rare in APS type 2.

faqs.question: What is autoimmune polyglandular failure?
faqs.answer: Autoimmune polyglandular failure (APS) is a condition where the immune system attacks and destroys two or more endocrine glands. APS type 1 presents in childhood with candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison disease. APS type 2 presents in adults with Addison disease, thyroid disease, and/or Type 1 diabetes.

faqs.question: Is APS type 1 hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. APS type 1 is autosomal recessive, caused by mutations in both copies of the AIRE gene. Both parents are carriers. APS type 2 has a polygenic inheritance pattern with multiple genetic risk factors (HLA, CTLA4) and familial clustering.

faqs.question: What is the difference between APS type 1 and type 2?
faqs.answer: APS type 1 is rare, childhood-onset, caused by AIRE gene mutations, and features the classic triad of chronic candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison disease. APS type 2 is more common, adult-onset, polygenic, and combines Addison disease with autoimmune thyroid disease and/or Type 1 diabetes.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of autoimmune polyglandular failure?
faqs.answer: Symptoms vary by which glands are affected but may include fatigue and hyperpigmentation (Addison disease), muscle cramps and tingling (hypoparathyroidism), oral white patches (candidiasis), cold intolerance and weight gain (hypothyroidism), and polyuria and thirst (Type 1 diabetes).

faqs.question: How is APS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires clinical evidence of two or more autoimmune endocrine deficiencies. Laboratory testing includes endocrine function tests (ACTH stimulation, TFTs, HbA1c, calcium/PTH) and autoantibody panels (anti-21-hydroxylase, anti-TPO, anti-GAD65). APS type 1 is confirmed by AIRE gene sequencing.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for APS?
faqs.answer: Treatment involves replacing all deficient hormones: hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for adrenal insufficiency, calcium and calcitriol for hypoparathyroidism, levothyroxine for hypothyroidism, insulin for Type 1 diabetes, and azoles for candidiasis. Regular surveillance for new deficiencies is essential.

faqs.question: What is an adrenal crisis in APS?
faqs.answer: An adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency where the body cannot produce enough cortisol, causing hypotension, confusion, vomiting, and shock. Patients with APS and adrenal insufficiency must always carry emergency hydrocortisone injection and wear a medical alert bracelet.

faqs.question: Can APS be cured?
faqs.answer: No. APS cannot be cured because the autoimmune destruction of endocrine glands is irreversible. Treatment requires lifelong hormone replacement for each deficient gland. Pancreatic islet cell transplantation and parathyroid transplantation are experimental approaches being researched.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with APS?
faqs.answer: With appropriate hormone replacement and monitoring, life expectancy is near-normal for most APS patients. APS type 1 has higher mortality in childhood due to adrenal crisis, hypocalcaemia, and infections. Good adherence to replacement therapy and sick-day protocols is critical.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested for APS?
faqs.answer: Yes. First-degree relatives of APS patients should be screened with autoantibody panels and baseline endocrine function tests. Relatives of APS type 1 patients can have AIRE gene testing. Annual surveillance screening is recommended for at-risk relatives.

faqs.question: What is hypoparathyroidism in APS?
faqs.answer: Hypoparathyroidism (low PTH) in APS type 1 causes low calcium and high phosphate, leading to muscle cramps, tingling in fingers and lips, tetany, and seizures. It is treated with calcium supplements and active vitamin D (calcitriol).

faqs.question: What causes chronic candidiasis in APS type 1?
faqs.answer: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis in APS type 1 results from immune dysregulation affecting the mucosal immune system. It causes persistent white oral plaques (thrush) and oesophageal candidiasis. Long-term azole antifungal therapy (itraconazole, fluconazole) is required.

faqs.question: Can APS affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: APS patients can have successful pregnancies with careful specialist management. Adrenal insufficiency requires stress-dose steroids in labour. Thyroid function requires close monitoring. Diabetes requires intensified insulin management. Multidisciplinary obstetric and endocrine care is essential.

faqs.question: What is the AIRE gene?
faqs.answer: The AIRE gene encodes a transcription factor that promotes central immune tolerance by triggering thymic expression of tissue-specific self-antigens. Mutations cause APS type 1 by allowing autoreactive T cells to escape deletion and attack endocrine tissues.

faqs.question: How many glands does APS typically affect?
faqs.answer: APS type 1 typically affects 3-5 glands or tissues (candidiasis, parathyroid, adrenal, gonad, pancreas, thyroid). APS type 2 typically involves 2-3 glands (adrenal, thyroid, pancreas). The number increases over time without treatment.

faqs.question: Are there other autoimmune conditions in APS?
faqs.answer: Yes. APS type 1 commonly includes vitiligo, alopecia, pernicious anaemia, autoimmune hepatitis, and gastritis. These are part of the spectrum of AIRE-dependent immune tolerance failure.

faqs.question: Is APS the same as multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN)?
faqs.answer: No. MEN syndromes (MEN1, MEN2A, MEN2B) involve tumorous hyperplasia or neoplasia of endocrine glands, not autoimmune destruction. APS involves autoimmune-mediated gland failure. They are entirely different pathophysiological entities.

faqs.question: Does stress trigger new glandular failures in APS?
faqs.answer: Stress, infection, and other physiological challenges do not directly cause new glandular failures in APS, but they can unmask subclinical deficiencies. New failures develop from ongoing autoimmune attack, not from external stressors.

faqs.question: Can patients with APS travel?
faqs.answer: Yes, with preparation. Carry emergency hydrocortisone injection, medical alert bracelet, letter from endocrinologist, extra medication supplies (2x what is needed), and know the local emergency medical contacts. Time zone changes require dose adjustment coordination with the specialist team.

faqs.question: What is the most dangerous aspect of APS?
faqs.answer: Adrenal crisis from adrenal insufficiency is the most immediately life-threatening complication. All patients with APS and adrenal insufficiency must be educated on sick-day dosing, emergency injection, and medical alert identification. It can be fatal within hours if untreated.

metaTitle: Autoimmune Polyglandular Failure: Types, Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: APS type 1 and type 2: Addison disease, thyroid disease, diabetes, hypoparathyroidism, AIRE gene. Treatment and prognosis. Medically reviewed guide.

Kahaly GJ, Frommer L. Autoimmune polyglandular syndromes. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2018;115(20):355-362.
